text: The video shows a robotic-assisted transaxillary thymectomy on a fourteen-year-old female patient. The patient is positioned supine with the left arm elevated above the head. A neural integrity monitoring endotracheal tube and an orogastric tube are visible. The surgical team marks the incision site by tracing a line from the sternal notch and cricoid process to the axilla.

The surgeon makes an incision down to the pectoralis fascia and creates a flap extending to the clavicle. Lighted retractors are used for enhanced visualization. Dissection proceeds into the platysma, identifying the sternocleidomastoid muscle. A plane is created between the sternal and clavicular heads of the sternocleidomastoid, with the sternal head retracted anteriorly. The omahyoid muscle is visible and may be divided for further exposure. A plane is carefully created underneath the strap muscles, avoiding injury to the internal jugular vein.

The surgeon exposes the thyroid gland by dissecting under the strap muscles, creating space for robotic-assisted dissection. A mounted retractor is placed beneath the strap muscles for robot docking. The vagus nerve is stimulated to confirm neural integrity, followed by identification and stimulation of the left recurrent laryngeal nerve.

The surgeon separates the thymus from the thyroid gland and opens the carotid sheath to identify the vagus nerve for continuous neural monitoring. The thymus is dissected off the trachea with care to avoid the recurrent laryngeal nerve, retracted cephalad, and delivered from the thorax. The thymus is then carefully dissected from the innominate vessels.
